speaker: interviewer
text: Shabilski interviewing Mike Popik. Mike, when I asked you to show us your number that's tattooed on your arm, you got some tears in your eyes. What was that about? What were you thinking or remembering at that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: That a thing like this saved my life. I mean, I had chances to die so many times after, in the war camp. But I had the right feeling about it, thanks to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he guided you.

speaker: interviewee
text: (WHISPERING) Yeah. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: He must've understood a lot even in that situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he probably knew. He probably knew that for a little boy, it's very important to leave this hell. Because otherwise, I wouldn't have too much chance. And we were taken to a small work camp near Sosnowitz. In German it was called [GERMAN]. It was a factory. I don't know what it was before the war, but it was transformed-- transformed to manufacturer, the flak, the anti-aircraft cannons for the German army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a small camp, about 1,400 people. And we were working there 12 hours a day. And I was a helper for my meister, from a professional who was working in a certain part of a machine. And I had to go always for tools. And I was helping him to bring the tools, and he give me all this what to do, and what to watch. And we had a little bit more food. The sanitation condition in this camp was a little bit better than Auschwitz. But it was a very hard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] labor camp. And the killings continued there, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the killings carried out there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if somebody was accused by sabotage, every Sunday they were hanging people there. Every Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the prisoners in this work camp, were they Jews? Or were there other people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in this camp, we were only Jews. But we worked together with French prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners of-- no. I don't believe they were prisoners of war as soldiers, but maybe from the resistance, or-- but prisoners. They wore the same clothing that we were, but they were in a different camp. In our camp, we were only Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike, did you see any of the boys that you were friends with in Auschwitz again?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this labor camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, either in the labor camp or later. Did you see any of those friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my close friends. In this labor camp, I became friends with two other boys from Slovakia, that we again became very good friends. Only one boy who was a little bit older from me, from the same city, Levice, Tibor Wise, he was with me all the time in the work camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as you know, the other boys also didn't survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Two of them survived with me together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Robert Vassarberger from Bratislava, and the other one was Gabi, and I can't remember his family name, from Czechoslovakia. But we became friends, and we were in this working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- also wanted to ask you your impression of Mengele, at least the times you saw him, or the last time. What was your impression of him, besides the fact that you were already scared of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were scared of him, but he didn't look nothing scary. Because he was a good-looking man. I mean, he was a good-looking guy, just to remember him. He had the-- we hated everything German. But he was a nice-looking man, tall and very strict. You know, he wasn't talking to us. I never heard talking to us, just when he came into the camp and said that who was working in metal. And I was afraid to lie because of his voice. But I had to lie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of his voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of his voice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, tone or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The tone. The tone, the way he was talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to me everything that was German, the tone was very frightening. So I don't know if he was different than others. But the German sound, the army, the people they were talking from, it was very frightening because I never experienced nothing similar. They were always yelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were many times you could have died. What would you attribute your survival to? What would you say helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first there was the feeling of surviving, to come home to meet again my family. I wanted to survive. I did everything possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought your mother and little brother were still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, definitely. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Definitely. I was rushing home from the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think there were some special skills, or luck, or some-- some confidence that you had that might have helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think pure luck. Nothing heroic. I didn't do anything except to steal some potato peels. I could've been shot just to steal potato peels. I could've been beaten. When I was called to the SS kitchen to clean potatoes, and I was hiding behind the belt slices of potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if a kapo would catch me with the slices of potatoes, or a German would catch me, one-- if he would hit me, we were weak, a strong something over the head, I would die. And many children died this way. And many elderly people died, also, this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was kicked by-- in Mauthausen I was kicked in my kidneys, and I lost 3/4 of my kidneys because of it. I could've died. And I could've died just from pure hunger or from diarrhea. In the war camp, I got diarrhea. You had to conceal that you have diarrhea because people with diarrhea, they were taken to Auschwitz. People with diarrhea had no chance to survive because we had no medicine. And diarrhea killed people. I was lucky. I was strong. And somehow I was chewing burned wood, which the Hungarian doctors told us-- the Hungarian prisoners. They were not practicing as doctors, but they were working with us. And they told us to chew burned wood. And maybe this helped to cure the diarrhea, and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you have to-- how long did you have to conceal the diarrhea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably four or five days. Four or five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm curious whether you were a rather independent child at home when you were still with your parents. Did your parents encourage you to be independent, or were you a little rebel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I was independent, no. I don't think. It just became. I think I became in the camp because of the necessity. So we were thinking day by day just to survive, to do something-- something to-- what was to survive, to grab-- to steal a piece of food somewhere if it was possibility. Or to put some extra clothing, to put some paper into the shoes so you didn't get frozen-- frostbite. I got-- for about 20 years, I couldn't feel this part of my foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of your foot couldn't you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, the big toe, and it was frostbitten in Mauthausen. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: You've-- oh, I'm sorry. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. So it was frostbitten, and it was aching and itching for many, many years. And somehow it just got well, and my toe is all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you don't think you're necessarily brave. But even when you mentioned speaking up to Mengele, actually lying, you were afraid, but you still did it. That was some kind of courage for a young boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it was. It was. It was, because I actually lied that I was working with metal. Where was a 13-year-old boy working metal? But also pure luck. If you don't mind, I tell you some story about we were emigrated from Mauthausen to Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the work camp-- from the work camp, how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was in the work camp till sometime in January 1945. Then the Russian army was very, very close. And we heard already the cannons. We didn't know it was the Russian army. We were evacuated from the war camps. We were taken to Sosnowitz to the train station, put in the boxcars, and taken to Mauthausen. Now, in the boxcars, the three days were just unbearable because the older people, they steal from us overnight the ration that we got for the three-day travel. So it was an impossible situation, the boxcar. We were three days. After three days traveling to Czechoslovakia, when the train stopped, we were able to jump from the train just for 10, 15 minutes, restroom and eat some snow. So for three days, I probably ate only snow and maybe some green stuff that was frozen below the snow-- anything. After three days, very late in the evening, the train arrived in Mauthausen. Mauthausen was a camp in the Alps, beautiful place. Absolutely-- even if it-- the camp was there, but the place was beautiful. I don't know why they built a camp over there. We arrived there late at night, and we were walking from the train station. It's-- I don't know exactly the distance, but it's about a half an hour by walking to the camp. We arrived to the camp, to Mauthausen, and it was a different look to this camp than I used to the other camps. This camp had tall, stone walls. And only above the stone was some barbed wire electric wires. We walked into this big camp and taken right away to the showers. And in the showers, I saw the most terrible, the most horrifying thing that a human being can experience. The kapos from Mauthausen camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were ordered to kill the kapos from our camp. Now, in our working camp, the kapos were fairly human. One of them was a very nice Czechoslovakian boy who even helped me when I got big pants and a big shirt. I always went to him and I told him, I am short and I'm small. I can't walk in those heavy. So he always tried to exchange me in the-- for a smaller-- for a smaller shirt and smaller [INAUDIBLE]. And this-- they were beating to death in the showers with like, baseball bats. Not baseball bats, but with sticks, beating them to death. And we had about 12 or 13 kapos in our camp. 14, I can't remember. And they were laying on the cement floor in the bathroom. We were scared to death because we couldn't understand from the beginning those animals who were beating them, literally breaking their bones-- not to hit them over the head so they should die right away. Just breaking them pieces. And you see human flesh and blood and human waste all over. It was the worst, worst picture that I-- [COUGHS] I saw people die. And I saw people taken almost-- they still breathing-- and taken away. Of course, we knew they're taking away to bury them. But here they were beating them to death. I just-- it was something very, very frightening, a picture that will never ever will forget. It was something unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know who the kapos were?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was an unwritten law, later I found out, that kapos from one camp shouldn't take over their jobs. So it was a-- it was a-- they were beat-- they knew exactly who were the kapos because they have list of people, and the Germans also gave them the list of the kapos. So they knew exactly who to grab and who to beat to death. One of them survived, just a miracle. One of them survived. His Polish friends helped him, and they bandaged him with paper bandages. But he hanged himself next day because he wouldn't be able to leave, anyway. It was all broken pieces. But he hanged himself and we walked out in the morning and I saw him. He hung himself on a rope-- not on a rope, on a belt. Maybe somebody assisted him, because wouldn't be able to do by himself. But he died, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that maybe someone else actually did it, or do you think he did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I think he wanted to die because, without medicine and without painkillers, the man was bleeding and broken. He wasn't able even to move his hands or his legs. So probably he was assisted to hang himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other instances of suicides in the camps or during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember people that died on the electric wire in Auschwitz. I don't know if they did it themselves, they jumped. But in the morning, we saw them just hanging on there. They were electrocuted.

speaker: interviewer
text: You often saw people on the barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, even in the work camps. Even in [NON-ENGLISH], we saw them. So that was all over everywhere. It wasn't nothing new to us. Mauthausen was a terrible, terrible camp, just with mean, mean German kapos. In Mauthausen were mainly German kapos. They were there for many years. They were convicted for different crimes, saboteurs, because they had different insignias that I never saw before, green and black. So we knew that they are different. And they were Germans, not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were the criminal element of German society?

speaker: interviewee
text: The criminal element. It was just disaster. They were beating people to death for absolutely nothing. We were 120 children in a barrack. And at night, we couldn't sleep. You know, it's very hard on the cement floors. So we were ordered on the snow, standing, midnight till 4 o'clock, or 5 o'clock in the morning. And from the beginning, we were jumping. And these wooden shoes got cold and frozen very, very fast. So we were jumping. And then, when the Germans saw that we are half frozen, they came. One of them was Kaduk. He was famous just like Mengele, but he was, to me, an animal because I saw him to kill people. And I didn't see Mengele to kill people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think his name was K-A-D-U-C-K, Kaduck. We know him by Ober-- Hauptscharfuhrer or Oberscharfuhrer. The exact Scharfuhrers I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wasn't a physician.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. He was in charge with Mauthausen, the camp. He was a tall, a mean-looking-- just an animal. And when he saw that we are almost frozen, then he gave us to exercise, to stand up, to get the blood to circulate. And my very, very good friend, Robert Vassarberger, he was bigger than I am-- maybe a little bit. But he was very, very fragile. He was always very fragile. And he was very weak. And I told him to grab my pants in the back, so I am able to help him to stand up, and then go back. And he came from the back, and he saw that Robert is holding me. And he approached me. I didn't see him because he came from the back. And he kicked me in the side. I flew to the other side of the room, but it was outside, 10 or 15 meters. Like a bull he kicked me. For three days, I wasn't able to breathe. I mean, very bad. And the pain was tremendous. But I tried to hide it. I was suffering for three days. I couldn't even eat because of it. But I made it. After three days, it-- I just 20 years later found out that my kidney was destroyed, 3/4 of it, because of this kick from the boot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has that given you trouble in your life, the problem with your kidney? Have you had effects from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for many years. The Israeli army tried-- they tried to find out, but they never told me the truth. I don't know why. I had lot of pains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it ever operated on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It just died by itself. In the early '70s, I found out here in the United States. And many other places we were evacuating from Mauthausen to Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go on to that, I wanted to ask you, during the beatings in the shower, what did the rest of you do? Were you afraid that next it would be your turn? Or what were the rest of you doing?

words.text: were the rest of you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. Exactly. From the beginning, we didn't know, when we saw their faces. Then we saw that one by one, those are our kapos. Even this nice Czech young kapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember what you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the rest of you had to watch this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to watch it. Very, very, afraid. Very frightening. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on. You were evacuated from Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So OK, we were in Mauthausen. The situation in Mauthausen was very, very bad, like I said, because of the German kapos. We were there till end of April. Before-- days before we were evacuated, we don't know. We didn't know then they dropped bombs on the camp. Now, we thought that they are the Germans. They wanted to destroy us. They bombed the camp. Bodies were flying all over. People were killed all over. And people were grabbing human flesh and cooking the human flesh. And this was also something that we children, we couldn't take it. Most of-- I don't remember that-- none of us from the young people, they took human flesh and cooked the human flesh. But the older, they took. I saw with my eyes that they grabbed from the wires human flesh, killed people, and they were cooking them. This  was almost end of the world. But from there, we were evacuated. I think they were evacuating Mauthausen to a forest. We were walking about three days, 60-odd kilometers from Mauthausen through the city of Linz to Gunskirchen. 60-some kilometers. On the second day, we were crossing a big bridge on the Donau. And we were about 3,000 in each transport, walking. 3,000 prisoners, maybe five or six German soldiers just watching us. We were walking. And we were on a bridge. I can't remember exactly the name, but I always see [NON-ENGLISH]. I saw the name [NON-ENGLISH] somewhere. And there were Hungarian, Romanian, and German soldiers coming from the other side of the bridge. And when I saw Hungarian soldiers, we were asking them, maybe they can throw us a piece of bread or something. And they looked very bad. And the Hungarian soldiers were yelling, dirty Jews, you are going to hell. You don't need nothing. I remember only that much of it. All of a sudden, they opened fire on us from machine guns of the German half trucks, you know, those Panther trucks. I saw fire. I remember the line, the line of the fire towards us. We were walking on the right side, and they were coming on the left. And I hit the bridge. And people were jumping into the water, yelling and crying. I just hit the bridge and people were falling on me. And it took maybe two or three minutes, and the-- and the shooting stopped. And I woke up. And I was alive, and people dead all over. And people jumped into the Donau. Of course they died. They drowned or died from the fall. And I woke up, and I was happy I am alive. And I saw the German soldier is waving that we should get together again. From the 3,000, most have-- less than the half survived. And we were walking again. And a day later we arrived in Gunskirchen. This was few wooden barracks. They were built new wood. I saw new wood. Huge barracks. And when we approached those barracks, I saw channels around those barracks. And I didn't like that there were no windows. Huge size of barracks-- just door and no windows. There were very few windows on the top. And when I walked into this barrack with my friends already that we became friends, like brothers during the camp, and during-- I told to my friends that I don't like it that there are no windows. So in the evening, I made plans already, if something happens, I can climb through those beams that are holding the barracks together to jump out of the window. It was claustrophobic. I didn't know what it-- I didn't know the word 'claustrophobia.'

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were already planning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just felt it, that I don't want to be in this barracks. Later on we found out that the intention was to pour some liquid into those channels and to burn those three or four barracks. I don't know how many were-- maybe they were more. I remember only three or four.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to change tapes.
